explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I was at work when we first heard about the Twin Towers going down. Shortly after, we felt a huge vibration and saw smoke near the Pentagon, which was terrifying. That day was the scariest of my life; I was overwhelmed with fear and uncertainty about what would happen next. We had to evacuate the park and cemetery, and I struggled to keep my composure while helping visitors. The whole experience changed how I hear the twenty-one-gun salutes now, as they remind me of that day.

explicit_text: Our location was right next to the Pentagon, so when the attack happened, we felt the impact and saw the huge smoke from the building. I ran outside to see what was going on and was shocked by the devastation. It was surreal and frightening to be so close to such a major event. The chaos around the Pentagon affected everyone nearby, including the people evacuating on foot. That day, the Pentagon was the center of our fear and uncertainty.

entity: Arlington House, The Robert E. Lee Memorial
explicit_text: During the evacuation on September 11, we were told to clear the Arlington House grounds as part of the larger effort to evacuate the Cemetery. Visitors were confused and some even asked if they could still tour the Robert E. Lee Home, which was surreal given the circumstances. We had to close the House and guide people off the premises, all while not having many answers ourselves. It was difficult to maintain order and calm during such an uncertain time.

explicit_text: On September 11, we had to evacuate Arlington National Cemetery after the Pentagon attack. It was a challenging task because we had no clear information and had to keep visitors calm and moving. The Cemetery, usually a place of peace, felt tense and uncertain as we worked with other staff and Tourmobile employees to clear the grounds. I was worried about safety and the unknown threats that day, making it one of the most difficult experiences in my job.

explicit_text: After the evacuation on September 11, I couldn't go home because all the bridges into Washington were shut down, so I stayed in Rosslyn for about four hours. I sat in my car, glued to the radio, trying to figure out the safest place to be amidst the chaos. It was a frightening and lonely time, especially without my family nearby. Rosslyn became a temporary refuge while the city was locked down.
